 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gt.8.3","title":"Simulate: output formats (pretty/json) + redaction/truncation","description":"## Objective

Implement `dcg simulate` output formatting in both human and machine forms.

## Pretty output requirements

- Summary header (totals + elapsed time)
- Top rules table:
  - rule_id
  - count
  - decision (deny/warn)
  - 1-line reason (if available)
- Optional section: sample commands per top rule (truncated + redacted)

## JSON output requirements

- `schema_version`
- totals
- `rules[]`: `{ rule_id, pack_id, decision, count, reason?, examples[] }`
- `errors[]`: parse errors counts, malformed lines count

## Privacy

- Default to truncation in examples.
- Support `--redact` consistent with scan/explain.

## Acceptance criteria

- Output is stable for golden tests.
- JSON output is valid and additive-only for schema v1.

## Dependencies

- `git_safety_guard-1gt.8.2` (aggregation)

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","title":"[PACK] remote.ssh - SSH Pack","description":"# SSH Remote Access Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for SSH operations, particularly remote command execution.

## Destructive Patterns
### Remote Command Execution
- `ssh host 'dangerous-command'` - Detect destructive commands executed remotely
- Piping destructive scripts through SSH
- Redirecting scripts into SSH

### Key Management
- `ssh-keygen -R host` - Removes known hosts (security implication)
- Deleting SSH keys

### Config Operations
- Modifying ~/.ssh/config destructively
- Removing authorized_keys entries

## Safe Patterns
- `ssh -V` (version)
- `ssh-keygen -l` (fingerprint display)
- `ssh-keyscan` (key scanning)
- `ssh host` (interactive session - user takes responsibility)
- `ssh host 'safe-command'` (non-destructive remote commands)

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: remote.* (git_safety_guard-g5o)
- Keywords: ssh, ssh-keygen
- CRITICAL: Must detect when OTHER destructive commands are tunneled through SSH

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests for remote delete commands via SSH
- [ ] Unit tests for piped script detection
- [ ] Unit tests for key management operations
- [ ] E2E test: `scripts/e2e_tests/remote_ssh.txt`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Remote destructive commands detected
- [ ] Key removal operations blocked
- [ ] Safe SSH usage allowed
- [ ] Interactive sessions not blocked","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:11:13.436955387-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T03:00:07.922675119-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-11T03:00:07.922675119-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented remote.ssh pack with 6 destructive patterns and 10 safe patterns; all 8 tests pass","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:44.132964887-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:44.177154661-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:44.223350479-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:44.270713954-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:44.316989212-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:44.363582267-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:13.365141001-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:13.41138624-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

## Parent Epic: Visual Explanations (git_safety_guard-r97e)

## Objective

Write detailed, helpful explanations for all destructive patterns in the core.filesystem pack. These explanations will be shown to users when commands are blocked.

## Patterns to Document

Review src/packs/core/filesystem.rs for all destructive patterns. Expected patterns include:
- Recursive deletion of root/home paths
- chmod 777 (world-writable permissions)  
- chown to root
- dd operations on block devices
- Filesystem format commands

## Explanation Template

For each pattern, provide:

1. WHAT: One sentence describing the destructive action
2. WHY DANGEROUS: What could go wrong (data loss, security risk, etc.)
3. ALTERNATIVE: Safer way to accomplish the goal
4. PREVIEW: Command to check before executing (if applicable)

## Example Explanation

Pattern: recursive-delete-root

Explanation: |
  This command would recursively delete files starting from the root
  or home directory. This is almost always catastrophic - it will
  destroy your operating system, all applications, and all user data.
  There is no recovery without backups.
  
  If you need to delete specific files, use explicit paths like:
    find /specific/path -name "pattern" -delete
  
  Always preview first with:
    find /specific/path -name "pattern" -print

## File Location

src/packs/core/filesystem.rs

## Acceptance Criteria

- Every destructive pattern in filesystem pack has explanation
- Explanations are concise but complete (2-4 sentences)
- At least one alternative suggested per pattern
- Preview commands where applicable

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-hcfg","title":"Add heredoc scanning configuration and CLI flags","description":"# Heredoc Scanning Configuration

## Objective

Allow users to configure heredoc scanning behavior via CLI flags and config file.

## CLI Flags

```
dcg check [OPTIONS]

Heredoc Scanning:
    --heredoc-scan          Enable heredoc scanning (default: true)
    --no-heredoc-scan       Disable heredoc scanning
    --heredoc-timeout <MS>  Timeout for heredoc analysis (default: 50)
    --heredoc-languages <L> Languages to scan (default: all)
                            Values: python,bash,javascript,typescript,ruby,perl
```

## Config File (~/.config/dcg/config.toml)

```toml
[heredoc]
enabled = true
timeout_ms = 50
languages = ["python", "bash", "javascript", "typescript", "ruby", "perl"]

# Graceful degradation
fallback_on_parse_error = true  # Allow if parsing fails
fallback_on_timeout = true       # Allow if timeout exceeded

# Logging
log_heredoc_detection = false    # Log when heredocs detected
log_pattern_matches = false      # Log which patterns matched
```

## Pack Integration

Add heredoc scanning as a pack that can be enabled/disabled:

```rust
// In packs/heredoc.rs
pub fn create_pack() -> Pack {
    Pack {
        id: "heredoc".to_string(),
        name: "Heredoc Scanning",
        description: "Detect destructive patterns in inline scripts and heredocs",
        keywords: &["<<", "-c", "-e", "python", "bash", "node"],
        safe_patterns: vec![],
        destructive_patterns: vec![],  // Patterns defined per-language
    }
}
```

## Environment Variables

- `DCG_HEREDOC_ENABLED=true|false`
- `DCG_HEREDOC_TIMEOUT=50`
- `DCG_HEREDOC_LANGUAGES=python,bash,javascript`

## Default Behavior

- Heredoc scanning: ENABLED by default
- Timeout: 50ms
- Languages: All supported
- Fallback: Allow on error (fail-open for usability)

## Test Cases

- Verify --no-heredoc-scan skips heredoc detection
- Verify timeout is respected
- Verify language filtering works
- Verify config file is loaded correctly
- Verify environment variables override config

## Acceptance Criteria

- CLI flags and config settings map to a single internal config model (no drift).
- Hook mode behavior is safe:
  - timeout/parse failures fail-open (allow) with optional diagnostics.
- `dcg explain` surfaces heredoc config decisions (enabled/disabled/timeout) for debugging.
- Unit tests cover flag parsing + config precedence (CLI > env > config file > defaults).

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-hcj","title":"Fix regex backtracking panic in normalize_command (discovered by fuzzing)","description":"## Summary

Fuzzer discovered a panic when `normalize_command` processes inputs with many consecutive `/` characters.

## Reproduction

```
//////////////////_(rm
```

or bytes with mixed `/` and null characters:
```
[47, 47, 47, 0, 0, 47, 0, 0, 47, 0, 0, 47, 47, 0, 0, 47, 0, 91, ...]
```

## Root Cause

In `src/packs/mod.rs:490`, `normalize_command` uses:

```rust
PATH_NORMALIZER.replace(cmd, "$1")
```

which internally calls `.unwrap()` on the regex result. When the regex engine hits its backtrack limit, this panics:

```
thread 'panicked at fancy-regex-0.14.0/src/lib.rs:1073:45:
called Result::unwrap() on an Err value: RuntimeError(BacktrackLimitExceeded)
```

## Fix

Handle the error gracefully by returning the original command unchanged:

```rust
pub fn normalize_command(cmd: &str) -> Cow<'_, str> {
    if !cmd.starts_with('/') {
        return Cow::Borrowed(cmd);
    }
    // Handle regex errors gracefully (fail-open)
    PATH_NORMALIZER.replace(cmd, "$1").unwrap_or(Cow::Borrowed(cmd))
}
```

## Artifacts

- `fuzz/artifacts/fuzz_evaluate/minimized-from-6116856d9c40a74797b32a4ad3efe166a30f82a7`
- `fuzz/artifacts/fuzz_normalize/minimized-from-c5521321fb46097edf17593ccf4ac7d3c9fed259`","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-08T00:54:15.525383593-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T00:58:43.724650343-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-08T00:58:43.724650343-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed: use try_replacen with fail-open behavior. Regression test added."}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-hcyz","title":"[NOTE] Tier Dependency Strategy Rationale","description":"# Tier Dependency Strategy Rationale

## Current Structure
```
Tier 1 (P0) - Critical Security Gaps
    ↓ (blocks)
Tier 2 (P1) - High Value
    ↓ (blocks)  
Tier 3 (P2) - Valuable
```

## Why Strict Sequential Dependencies?

### Arguments FOR strict ordering (current approach):
1. **Quality gates**: Completing Tier 1 fully ensures critical security coverage before expanding
2. **Testing validation**: Tier 1 implementation validates our testing infrastructure
3. **Pattern refinement**: Lessons learned from Tier 1 improve Tier 2/3 implementation
4. **Focus**: Prevents spreading too thin across 57 packs simultaneously
5. **Confidence building**: Fully tested Tier 1 builds confidence in the approach

### Arguments AGAINST strict ordering:
1. **Flexibility**: Different teams might need different packs sooner
2. **Bottleneck**: One slow Tier 1 pack blocks all Tier 2/3 work
3. **Parallelization**: Could work on multiple tiers simultaneously

## Decision: Keep Strict Ordering

**Rationale**: The quality and testing benefits outweigh the flexibility costs. This is a safety-critical system where incomplete coverage is dangerous. Better to have 20 fully-tested, production-ready packs than 57 partially-tested packs.

## Escape Hatch
If a specific Tier 2/3 pack is urgently needed:
1. Create a separate task with elevated priority
2. Document the business justification
3. Ensure full testing before merge
4. Don't count it toward tier completion

## Review Point
After Tier 1 completion, evaluate:
- Did strict ordering help or hinder?
- Should Tier 2/3 be parallelized?
- What process improvements are needed?","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:35:22.900079048-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T01:53:42.705596148-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T01:53:42.705596148-05:00","close_reason":"Documented tier dependency strategy rationale and escape hatch in docs/tier-dependency-strategy.md"}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-hewe","title":"[Span] Create terminal formatter for caret highlighting","description":"# Task: Create terminal formatter for caret highlighting

## Parent Epic: Span Highlighting (git_safety_guard-jpfm)

## Objective

Create a reusable formatter that renders commands with caret-style highlighting showing exactly which portion matched.

## Example Output

Input command: git reset --hard HEAD
Match span: bytes 0..16

Output:
  git reset --hard HEAD
  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  └── Matched: git reset --hard

## Implementation Steps

1. Create new module src/highlight.rs (or extend src/trace.rs)
2. Implement format_with_highlight(command, spans) function
3. Handle ANSI color codes for terminal highlighting
4. Support multiple spans (compound commands)
5. Handle non-TTY gracefully (no colors, simplified format)
6. Handle UTF-8 correctly (byte offsets to char display)

## API Design

pub struct HighlightSpan {
    pub start: usize,       // byte offset
    pub end: usize,         // byte offset  
    pub label: Option<String>, // optional annotation
}

pub fn format_highlighted_command(
    command: &str,
    spans: &[HighlightSpan],
    use_color: bool,
) -> String;

## Edge Cases

- Multiple non-overlapping spans
- Overlapping spans (pick primary)
- Span at end of command
- Very long commands (truncation with ellipsis)
- Multi-line commands

## Acceptance Criteria

- Caret line aligns correctly with match
- Colors work in terminals that support them
- Graceful fallback for non-TTY
- Works with UTF-8 characters

    use_color: bool,
) -> String;

## Edge Cases

- Multiple non-overlapping spans
- Overlapping spans (pick primary)
- Span at end of command
- Very long commands (truncation with ellipsis)
- Multi-line commands

## Acceptance Criteria

- Caret line aligns correctly with match
- Colors work in terminals that support them
- Graceful fallback for non-TTY
- Works with UTF-8 characters

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-hi1t","title":"[TASK] Production monitoring and observability for packs","description":"# Production Monitoring and Observability for Packs

## Problem
Once packs are deployed, we need to know:
- Are packs actually blocking dangerous commands?
- Are there false positives frustrating users?
- Which packs are most used/triggered?
- Are there patterns that never match (dead code)?

## Deliverables

### 1. Opt-In Telemetry
User-configurable telemetry (OFF by default):
```toml
[telemetry]
enabled = true
anonymize = true  # Hash command content
endpoint = 'https://dcg.dev/telemetry'
```

Collected metrics:
- Pack name triggered
- Pattern ID matched
- Block/allow decision
- Command hash (not content)
- Timestamp

### 2. Local Statistics
`dcg stats` command showing local history:
```
Pack Statistics (last 30 days):
  secrets.vault:     47 blocks, 0 allows, 2 bypasses
  backup.rclone:     12 blocks, 0 allows, 1 bypass
  Total:             142 blocks, 23 allows, 5 bypasses

Most triggered patterns:
  1. vault secrets disable (23 times)
  2. rsync --delete (12 times)
  3. aws s3 rb (8 times)
```

### 3. False Positive/Negative Tracking
Quick feedback mechanism:
```bash
# After a block, user can report
dcg feedback --false-positive 'vault list secret/'
dcg feedback --false-negative 'vault kv destroy secret/prod'
```

### 4. Health Check Endpoint
For CI/monitoring integration:
```bash
dcg health
# Returns: OK, pack count, pattern count, last update
```

### 5. Dead Pattern Detection
Identify patterns that never match:
```bash
dcg analyze --unused-patterns
# Reports patterns with 0 matches in production
```

## Privacy Considerations
- Command content NEVER sent to telemetry
- Only hashes and metadata
- User must explicitly opt-in
- Local stats stay local

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Telemetry system designed (opt-in)
- [ ] Local statistics command implemented
- [ ] Feedback mechanism implemented
- [ ] Health check endpoint available
- [ ] Dead pattern detection available
- [ ] Privacy review completed","notes":"Testing/Validation:
- Unit tests for metrics/log formatting and redaction behavior.
- Integration test to ensure telemetry can be enabled/disabled deterministically.
- E2E harness captures sample log/metric emission with verbose logging for audit.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:56:58.284194805-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T04:42:47.145998764-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-11T04:23:57.680825855-05:00"}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-qdhh","title":"[DESIGN] GitHub/GitLab Pack Scope Delineation","description":"# GitHub/GitLab Pack Scope Delineation

## Problem
We have overlapping packs that both cover `gh` and `glab` commands:
- `platform.github` / `platform.gitlab` - Broad platform operations
- `cicd.github_actions` / `cicd.gitlab_ci` - CI/CD specific operations

## Proposed Resolution

### Merge Approach (RECOMMENDED)
Merge the platform and cicd packs for each provider:
- `github.*` single pack covering all `gh` commands
- `gitlab.*` single pack covering all `glab` commands

**Rationale**: 
- Users think in terms of 'GitHub commands' not 'platform vs CI'
- Simpler mental model
- No risk of pattern duplication or gaps
- Easier to maintain

### Implementation
1. Close `platform.github` (git_safety_guard-dxs) and `platform.gitlab` (git_safety_guard-h5j) categories
2. Close `platform.github` (git_safety_guard-3w5) and `platform.gitlab` (git_safety_guard-z2q) pack tasks
3. Rename `cicd.github_actions` to `github` and expand scope
4. Rename `cicd.gitlab_ci` to `gitlab` and expand scope
5. Move expanded packs to appropriate category (or create new `vcs.*` category)

## Decision Required
- [ ] Team agrees on merge approach
- [ ] Update affected beads
- [ ] Document in architecture decisions

-{"id":"git_safety_guard-qqsl","title":"[Explain] Write explanations for core.git patterns","description":"# Task: Write explanations for core.git patterns

## Parent Epic: Visual Explanations (git_safety_guard-r97e)

## Objective

Write detailed explanations for all destructive patterns in the core.git pack. Git commands are frequently blocked by DCG, so these explanations are high-visibility.

## Patterns to Document

Review src/packs/core/git.rs for patterns including:
- git reset --hard
- git checkout -- (file)
- git clean -f
- git push --force  
- git branch -D
- git stash drop/clear
- git rebase (history rewrite)

## Example Explanations

### reset-hard
Explanation: |
  git reset --hard discards all uncommitted changes in your working
  directory and staging area. This cannot be undone - changes that
  were never committed are permanently lost.

  Safer alternatives:
  - git stash: Saves changes for later retrieval
  - git reset --soft: Keeps changes in working directory
  - git reset --mixed: Keeps changes unstaged (default)

  Preview what would be lost:
    git diff && git status

### push-force  
Explanation: |
  Force pushing overwrites remote history, potentially destroying
  commits that others have already pulled. This can cause data loss
  for your entire team and requires manual recovery.

  Safer alternative:
  - git push --force-with-lease: Only forces if no one else pushed

  Check remote state first:
    git fetch && git log origin/branch..HEAD

## File Location

src/packs/core/git.rs

## Acceptance Criteria

- Every git destructive pattern has explanation
- Explanations mention data loss consequences
- All patterns have safer alternatives
- Preview commands for checking state

### 1. Performance Benchmark Suite

```rust

fn bench_single_pack_evaluation(c: &mut Criterion) {
    // Benchmark each pack individually
}

    // Benchmark with all packs enabled
}

fn bench_pattern_matching(c: &mut Criterion) {
    // Benchmark regex pattern matching specifically
}
```

### 2. Performance Budgets
Define maximum acceptable times:
- Single pack evaluation: < 500µs
- All packs evaluation: < 5ms
- Regex pattern match: < 100µs per pattern
- Total hook execution: < 10ms

### 3. CI Performance Tracking
- Run benchmarks on every PR
- Compare against baseline
- Fail PR if regression > 10%
- Track performance over time (grafana/dashboard)

### 4. Performance Logging
When DEBUG enabled:
- Log time per pack
- Log time per pattern
- Identify slow patterns
- Suggest optimizations

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Benchmark suite covers all performance-critical paths
- [ ] Budgets defined and enforced in CI
- [ ] Performance tracked over time

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","title":"[PACK] platform.gitlab - GitLab Platform Pack","description":"# GitLab Platform Pack (Non-CI Operations)

## Scope Clarification
This pack covers GitLab **platform-wide** operations that are NOT covered by `cicd.gitlab_ci`:
- Project/repository management
- Releases
- Protected branches
- Webhooks
- Group management

**The `cicd.gitlab_ci` pack covers:**
- CI/CD variables
- Pipelines
- Runners

## Destructive Patterns (Platform-specific)

### Project Operations
- `glab repo delete` - Project deletion
- `glab repo archive` - Archive project

### Release Operations
- `glab release delete` - Deletes releases

### API Operations
- `glab api -X DELETE projects/` - Project deletion
- `glab api -X DELETE .../protected_branches/` - Remove protection
- `glab api -X DELETE .../hooks/` - Webhook deletion

### Admin Operations (self-hosted)
- `gitlab-rails runner` with destructive code
- `gitlab-rake` destructive maintenance tasks

## Safe Patterns
- `glab repo view`, `glab repo list`, `glab repo clone`
- `glab mr list`, `glab mr view`
- `glab issue list`, `glab issue view`
- `glab api` with GET method

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: platform.gitlab (git_safety_guard-h5j)
- Keywords: glab, gitlab-rails, gitlab-rake
- IMPORTANT: Do NOT duplicate patterns from cicd.gitlab_ci

- [ ] E2E tests in `scripts/e2e_tests/platform_gitlab.txt`
- [ ] Verify no overlap with cicd.gitlab_ci patterns
- [ ] Test project/release operations
- [ ] Performance < 500µs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Project deletion blocked
- [ ] Release deletion blocked
- [ ] Protected branch removal blocked
- [ ] No duplicate patterns with cicd.gitlab_ci

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-z72c","title":"Allow-once codes: Switch from hex to numeric-only format","notes":"
---

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests

```rust
#[test]
fn test_numeric_code_generation() {
    // Generate 1000 codes and verify all are 5-digit numeric
    for _ in 0..1000 {
        let code = generate_bypass_code();
        assert_eq!(code.len(), 5);
        assert!(code.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_digit()));
    }
}

#[test]
fn test_code_uniqueness() {
    // Generate 10000 codes and check collision rate
    let mut codes = std::collections::HashSet::new();
    for _ in 0..10000 {
        codes.insert(generate_bypass_code());
    }
    // With 100000 possible codes, expect <1% collision rate
    assert!(codes.len() > 9900);
}

#[test]
fn test_code_validation() {
    assert!(is_valid_bypass_code("12345"));
    assert!(is_valid_bypass_code("00000"));
    assert!(is_valid_bypass_code("99999"));
    assert!(!is_valid_bypass_code("1234"));   // Too short
    assert!(!is_valid_bypass_code("123456")); // Too long
    assert!(!is_valid_bypass_code("1234a"));  // Contains letter
    assert!(!is_valid_bypass_code("abcde"));  // All letters
}

#[test]
fn test_code_storage_and_lookup() {
    let store = PendingExceptions::new_temp();
    let code = store.add_exception("test command", Duration::from_secs(3600));

    assert!(store.validate_and_consume(&code));
    assert!(!store.validate_and_consume(&code)); // Can only use once
}
```

### Integration Tests

```rust
#[test]
fn test_bypass_flow_with_numeric_code() {
    // 1. Block a command
    let result = evaluate_command("dangerous command");
    assert_eq!(result.outcome, Outcome::Deny);

    // 2. Get the bypass code from output
    let code = result.bypass_code.unwrap();
    assert!(code.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_digit()));

    // 3. Use code to bypass
    let bypass_result = bypass_with_code(&code);
    assert!(bypass_result.is_ok());
}
```

### E2E Tests

```bash
# Test numeric code is displayed on block
OUTPUT=$(echo '{"command": "dangerous"}' | dcg hook --stdin 2>&1)
CODE=$(echo "$OUTPUT" | grep -oE '[0-9]{5}')
[[ ${#CODE} -eq 5 ]] || exit 1
echo "PASS: 5-digit numeric code displayed"

# Test code can be used for bypass
dcg bypass "$CODE" && echo "PASS: Bypass accepted" || exit 1

## Parent Epic: Visual Explanations (git_safety_guard-r97e)

## Objective

Display pattern explanations in both hook denial output and dcg explain/test commands, providing users with context about why their command was blocked.

## Integration Points

### 1. Hook Denial Output (src/hook.rs)

Add explanation section to the denial box:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ⛔ BLOCKED by DCG                                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Pattern: core.git:reset-hard                                │
│                                                             │
│ Why blocked:                                                │
│   git reset --hard discards all uncommitted changes.        │
│   Changes that were never committed are permanently lost.   │
│                                                             │
│ Suggestions:                                                │
│   • Use git stash to save changes first                     │
│   • Use git reset --soft to preserve changes                │
│   • Preview: git diff && git status                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

### 2. dcg explain Command (src/cli.rs)

The explain command already shows detailed information. Enhance with:
- Full explanation text
- Structured suggestions list
- Documentation links

### 3. dcg test Command

Brief version of explanation for quick testing.

## Implementation Steps

1. Modify hook denial formatter to include explanation
2. Update dcg explain to show full explanation
3. Update dcg test to show brief explanation
4. Handle missing explanations gracefully (show nothing extra)
5. Ensure formatting fits terminal width

## Text Wrapping

Explanations may be multiple lines. Use textwrap crate or similar to wrap at terminal width (default 80 columns).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Hook denials show explanation when available
- dcg explain shows full explanation with suggestions
- Missing explanations handled gracefully
- Text wraps correctly at terminal width

## Dependencies

- Task: Add explanation field to DestructivePattern
- At least some pattern explanations written
